notes: Launch from Alviso Marina into tidal sloughs. Tidal range runs 9-10 feet, so push off about an hour before low. Floating dock; gates close at sunset.
geocode_display: Alviso Marina, Alviso, San Jose, Santa Clara County, California, 95002, United States

notes: Human-powered craft only, so the water stays flat and calm. Launch fee is $5 total ($3 launch, $2 inspection), payable online in advance.
geocode_display: Stevens Creek Reservoir, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: Wide open reservoir with enough fetch to catch an afternoon breeze. Power boats share the water, so expect wake near the ramp. Arrive early on warm weekends.
geocode_display: Coyote Lake County Park Boat Launch, 10840 Coyote Lake Road, Gilroy, CA 95020

notes: Glassy on calm mornings. Power boats banned, so conditions stay predictable.
geocode_display: Lexington Reservoir County Park, 17770, Los Gatos, Santa Clara County, California, 95032, United States

notes: No outside watercraft allowed; you paddle the concessionaire's rentals.
geocode_display: Vasona Lake County Park, 333, Shannon, Los Gatos, Santa Clara County, California, 95032, United States

notes: Power boats prohibited and PFDs required. Wind picks up mid-afternoon, so morning sessions are best.
geocode_display: Shoreline Lake, Mountain View, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: Calm, traffic-free water across 2,000 acres of pickleweed marsh, good for building bay skills. NE winds of 10-15 mph are typical by afternoon, so start early.
geocode_display: Palo Alto Baylands Nature Preserve (manual)

notes: Tree-lined banks buffer the wind most days. Power boats allowed, so expect wake on busy afternoons. Launch fee is $5 ($3 launch, $2 inspection).
geocode_display: Calero County Park (Closed Area), San Jose, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: Splits into two sections. Upper Lake is paddle-craft only, no motors, open April through October. Lower Lake has a boat ramp and boat-in campsites, open year-round. Lots fill before 11 AM in summer.
geocode_display: Lake Clementine, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Multiple coves and timber shoreline, with launch access at Orchard Springs, Greenhorn, and Long Ravine campgrounds. Day-use launch fees run around $22-26 depending on the ramp.
geocode_display: Rollins Lake Road, Shady Glen, Placer County, CA

notes: Clear water over granite boulders just south of Incline Village. Entry is by timed reservation mid-April through mid-October ($10-15 per vehicle), plus a $20-25 launch fee.
geocode_display: Sand Harbor Beach, Washoe County, Nevada

notes: At 5,600 feet in Stanislaus National Forest, a compact 300-acre reservoir with a 20-mph boat speed limit and no personal watercraft. Calm mornings, cool mountain air.
geocode_display: Pinecrest Lake Resort, 421, Pinecrest Lake Road, Strawberry, Tuolumne County, California, 95364, United States

notes: Forested shores and calm water at Sly Park Recreation Area. Day-use entry is $13 per vehicle, $15 on holidays.
geocode_display: Jenkinson Lake, Sly Park, El Dorado County, California, United States

notes: Same blue water as Tahoe with far less boat traffic. Non-motorized launch fee is $5, and TRPA requires a current aquatic invasive species inspection before you put in.
geocode_display: Fallen Leaf Lake Store, 400, Fallen Leaf Road, Fallen Leaf, South Lake Tahoe, El Dorado County, California, 96150, United States

notes: Carnelian Bay hub on the north shore: dog-friendly beach and a cafe serving breakfast and lunch until 3 PM. Water is Tahoe-clear, facing west toward the Sierra crest.
geocode_display: Lake Tahoe, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Sheltered from Bay chop by Municipal Pier, the calmest open-water launch in SF. A natural starting point toward Ghirardelli Square or the piers. Water stays in the low 50s-60s, so a wetsuit is strongly recommended.
geocode_display: Aquatic Cove, San Francisco, California, 94123, United States

notes: Sheltered by the dry docks to the south and Pier 50 to the north, so it stays calmer than most of the SF waterfront. Expect harbor seals and herons. No swimming due to submerged industrial debris.
geocode_display: Crane Cove Park Beach, 699 Illinois St, San Francisco, CA 94107

notes: Launch from the ADA-accessible floating dock at Mission Creek Park, 10-15 minutes from McCovey Cove with Bay Bridge views the whole way. The creek is sheltered and nearly current-free. On Giants game days the cove gets crowded with ferries and spectator boats, so stay alert outside the creek channel. Pier 52 at Bayfront Park, just south in Mission Bay, is San Francisco's public launch ramp, with two boat ramps, an ADA low-float dock, and trailer parking. Pier 40 at South Beach Harbor is another nearby put-in and home to the City Kayak outfitter.
geocode_display: Mission Creek Kayak Launch, 401 Berry St, San Francisco, CA 94158

notes: Immediate access to the upper reservoir arms, where canyon walls keep wind modest. Arrive early on summer weekends before parking fills.
geocode_display: Folsome Lake Black Miners Bar State Park, Represa, Folsom, Sacramento County, California, United States

notes: Launch points at Peninsula Campground, Rattlesnake Bar, Granite Beach, Browns Ravine, and Beeks Bite. Afternoon wind builds fast on the main lake. Check which ramps are open seasonally, since water levels fluctuate a lot.
geocode_display: Folsom Lake, Folsom, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Calm, motor-free water with a 5-mph speed limit, very beginner-friendly. Launch from Leo J. Ryan Park or the Boat Park near the dog park at the south end.
geocode_display: Foster City, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Extensive breakwaters keep the harbor calm even when the open ocean is rough. Launch from the public side of the Half Moon Bay Yacht Club. Sea lions and harbor seals haul out near the yacht club.
geocode_display: Pillar Point Harbor Beach, Half Moon Bay, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: The Chesapeake Drive ramp at Redwood City Municipal Marina puts you on Redwood Creek with access to the Bay Water Trail and open bay. Large commercial ships use the main channel, so paddle inside the sloughs rather than near the shipping lane. Restrooms in the marina building.
geocode_display: Chesapeake Drive Boat Ramp, 601 Chesapeake Dr, Redwood City, CA 94063

notes: Tidal channels wind through 3,000 acres of salt marsh with dense wildlife: harbor seals in spring, shorebirds on the Pacific Flyway. Stick to mid or high tide or you'll bottom out in the muck before reaching the inner sloughs.
geocode_display: Redwood Creek, Redwood City, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Free public launch at the far left end of the private marina's parking lot. From here you can reach open Bay or thread the nearby sloughs.
geocode_display: Westpoint Harbor, 101, Westpoint Harbor Drive, Pacific Shores Center, Redwood City, San Mateo County, California, 94063, United States

notes: Former landfill peninsula with a dedicated kayak and windsurf launch next to Ryder Park. Open San Francisco Bay water with moderate tidal current. Easy parking and a paved path along the water.
geocode_display: Seal Point Park, San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Westside Regional Park has a three-lane boat ramp and a wheelchair-accessible kayak launch. Well-protected from open Pacific swell, manageable even when the coast is rough. Open daily until sunset.
geocode_display: Bodega Bay, Sonoma County, California, 94923, United States

notes: Protected tidal creek past Larkspur's houseboats that stays calm in wind. Launch from Bon Air Landing Park or Piper Park; paddle upstream past the Bon Air Road bridge at high tide. Tidal, so check the chart and go with the flow.
geocode_display: Corte Madera Creek, Larkspur, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Year-round beach launch at Steelhead Beach Regional Park. Summer flow runs 3-5 knots and is friendly for recreational boards; spring flows can hit 8-10 knots after rain, so check conditions. The lot fills fast on summer weekends.
geocode_display: Steelhead Beach, Forestville, Sonoma County

water: Russian River - Sunset Beach River Park
notes: Sunset Beach River Park, locally called Mom's Beach, is a wide sandy stretch backed by redwoods, a short walk from parking. A midpoint on the 2-hour Steelhead-to-Sunset float. Free loaner life vests on the beach in summer; weekend shuttle Memorial Day through Labor Day.
geocode_display: Sunset Beach River Park, Forestville, Sonoma County

notes: 50 miles of shoreline with almost no visible development. Launch at Yorty Creek (car-top only) or the marina ramp near the dam ($3-20). Western coves are most sheltered; midlake wind builds by noon.
geocode_display: Yorty Creek Recreation Area, Lake Sonoma, Cloverdale, CA

notes: A seasonal summer dam creates flat, lake-like conditions. On-site rentals from Russian River Tubes and Kings Russian River, mid-June through late September. Weekday mornings are peaceful; summer weekends bring crowds.
geocode_display: Guerneville, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Lines the Russian River just outside downtown, with two oxbow lakes inside the park for quiet, no-motor paddling off the main river. Reaching the lakes takes a short walk with your board.
geocode_display: Healdsburg, Sonoma County, California, 95448, United States

notes: Healdsburg Veterans Memorial Beach: broad sandy beach, lifeguards in season, easy access on a gentle Class I stretch. Season runs May through September. Afternoon winds funnel through the valley, so mornings are smoothest.
geocode_display: Healdsburg Veterans Memorial Beach, Old Redwood Highway, Healdsburg, Sonoma County, California, 95448, United States

notes: Inside Tomales Bay State Park facing the protected inner bay, which keeps the water calm and the launch manageable. Blue Waters Kayaking stages gear on-site on weekends in season. Afternoon winds pick up fast, so aim for morning. The lot fills before noon on sunny days.
geocode_display: Heart's Desire Beach, Marin County, California, United States

notes: A 25-mile reservoir with dramatic canyon topography and little congestion outside peak summer holidays. Launch free at the Putah Creek car-top access, or use Capell Cove or Markley Cove ramps for a small fee. Deep, clear, and exposed to afternoon valley wind, so paddle mornings.
geocode_display: Lake Berryessa, Napa County, California, United States

notes: Narrow, protected waters running 15 miles along the San Andreas Fault. Launch from Miller Boat Launch on the east shore: two-lane ramp, $5 parking. Watch for afternoon NW winds and opposing tides mid-bay near Hog Island, where chop builds quickly.
geocode_display: Miller Boat Launch, 23240 CA-1, Marshall, CA 94940

notes: ADA-accessible gangway and low-freeboard dock drop-in at Pickleweed Inlet on Richardson Bay. Calm tidal paddling through marsh channels rich with birdlife. Unusable at low tide when mudflats extend into the inlet, so check tides before arriving.
geocode_display: Bayfront Park, Alto, Mill Valley, Marin County, California, United States

notes: A mellow tidal stretch through downtown with winery-flanked banks and almost no boat traffic. Launch from the Jim Hench Memorial Kayak Launch at Oxbow Commons or the Main Street Dock. Mornings are calmest; afternoon NW winds can push upriver for a downwind run back.
geocode_display: Kennedy Park Boat Launch, Napa, CA 94559

notes: Quiet tule-marsh estuary launching from Stafford Lake Park. The creek grows more tidal toward San Pablo Bay, so plan for a mid-to-high tide to keep water under your board.
geocode_display: Novato Creek, Novato, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Two put-ins on the same tidal river. The Petaluma Marina near Hwy 101 has a 24-hour public boat ramp with ample parking. Upstream, the Petaluma Basin has a floating dock beside the old River House on Weller Street. Calm, ideal for mellow out-and-back sessions through marshland. Stick to mornings; afternoon NW winds can make the return a slog. In July, time it with Bands on the Basin for live music on the water.
geocode_display: Petaluma River, Petaluma, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: A pristine tidal estuary in Point Reyes with glassy water, harbor seals, and almost no powerboats. Launch is 0.7 miles down a dirt road off Sir Francis Drake Blvd near the Schooner Creek bridge. Closed March 1 through June 30 for seal pupping; otherwise time mid-to-high tide to avoid stranding on mudflats.
geocode_display: Drakes Estero, Marin County, California, 94937, United States

notes: Launch from the gravel beach at China Camp Village at mid or high tide. At low tide the mud is deep and sticky and you can get stuck, so always check the tide chart.
geocode_display: China Camp Village Beach, China Camp State Park, San Rafael, CA

notes: Best accessed at Buck's Landing, a small Marin County park with free parking and a shore launch. The creek winds through protected marsh. Current picks up near the bay mouth, so watch conditions on the outbound leg.
geocode_display: Buck's Landing, Gallinas Creek, San Rafael, CA

notes: A long, narrow sandy beach on the Bay Water Trail with easy access to San Pablo Bay. Launching is a carry-in off the sand, not a paved ramp. The county park charges an entrance fee. Mudflats extend far offshore at low tide, making launch and landing genuinely difficult: come at mid or high tide and watch for strong currents near the pier.
geocode_display: McNears Beach County Park, San Rafael, Marin County

notes: Quiet urban put-in on a small waterway in the Sonoma County creek system. Calm and sheltered for a relaxed out-and-back. Water can run low in late summer, so check conditions first.
geocode_display: Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Motor-limited lake with a year-round paved ramp at the Newanga Avenue entrance. Rentals through Sonoma County Parks, Memorial Day through Labor Day. Life jackets required.
geocode_display: Spring Lake Park, Melita, Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Go-to launch for Sausalito's waterfront and Richardson Bay, with a small ADA-accessible beach steps from the water. Paddle east to Angel Island (about 3 miles) or north along the waterfront. Watch for afternoon westerlies that make the return a push.
geocode_display: Dunphy Park, Sausalito, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Launch beneath the Marin tower of the Golden Gate Bridge at Fort Baker, with free parking right at the water's edge. Currents at the Gate can hit 6 knots on a strong ebb, so check the tide tables before heading outside the cove.
geocode_display: Horseshoe Cove / East Fort Baker, Sausalito, CA

notes: Broad, shallow arm of the Bay between Sausalito and Tiburon with protected water. Launch from Dunphy Park or Schoonmaker Point. Roughly 900 acres of the southern bay fall within an Audubon Sanctuary with seasonal closures for migratory birds.
geocode_display: Turney Street Public Boat Ramp, Sausalito, CA 94965

notes: Launch from Dunphy Park at high tide, or from a marina boat ramp for a flatter entry. Wind can funnel hard through the Gate on spring and summer afternoons, so go mornings for glassy water between Sausalito and the Marin headlands.
geocode_display: Schoonmaker Beach, Sausalito, CA 94965

hidden_reason: Coordinate reverse-geocodes to Kelly Road, Cloverdale (~30km north of the Guerneville/Healdsburg put-ins its notes name); untrustworthy pin. Guerneville Russian River is covered by spot 150 (Guerneville River Park). Un-hide only if re-pointed to a sourced put-in (e.g. Johnson's Beach or Veterans Memorial Beach) per D26.
notes: Lazy summer paddling through redwood-lined banks at Guerneville and Healdsburg, with put-ins at Johnson's Beach and Veterans Memorial Beach. Best June through early October when the river runs low and clear. Ask outfitters about depth before a long stretch in August.
geocode_display: Russian River, California, United States

notes: Two free parking lots and a small beach opening onto Richardson Bay, with views to San Francisco and Angel Island. Part of the bay falls within the Audubon Sanctuary, which restricts watercraft during migratory bird season, so verify the boundary before launching.
geocode_display: Blackie's Pasture Beach, Tiburon Boulevard, Tiburon, CA

notes: Strawberry Point juts into Richardson Bay with access to sheltered water from a dock at the end of E. Strawberry Drive. Beginner-friendly and shielded from direct Gate wind. Stay clear of the Audubon Sanctuary boundary in the southern bay during closure season.
geocode_display: Tiburon Boulevard & East Strawberry Drive, Tiburon Boulevard, Bel Aire, Marin County, California, 94925, United States

notes: Narrow, sheltered channel between Alameda and Oakland with almost no swell and minimal powerboat traffic. Multiple street-end access points make it easy to drop in. Reliable flat water year-round.
geocode_display: Oakland Marinas - Oakland Alameda Estuary, Embarcadero West, Jack London Square, Downtown Oakland, Oakland, Alameda County, California, 94607, United States

notes: Half-mile sandy shoreline with a gentle beach launch. At low or minus tide the beach flattens out and launching becomes very difficult, so plan around mid to high tide. Wind and chop pick up in the afternoon. Busy on summer weekends.
geocode_display: Robert W. Crown Memorial State Beach, Alameda, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: A quiet Alameda shoreline with direct Bay access, sheltered on calm days. The beach exposes at low or minus tide and launch becomes impractical, so aim for mid to high tide. Informal beach launch with street parking nearby.
geocode_display: Encinal beach, Alameda, Alameda County, California, United States

water: Encinal Boat Ramp to Ballena Bay
notes: Clean paved launch into the protected inner estuary. Paddle north along Crown Beach or southeast into Ballena Bay's calm marina waters, well sheltered from the open Bay. Usable at all tide levels.
geocode_display: Encinal Boat Ramp, Alameda County

notes: Wide sandy shore behind the Albany Bulb, a semi-sheltered basin that softens Bay chop on calmer days. ADA parking and restrooms on site. Avoid the Albany Mudflats Ecological Reserve to the north, a state marine park closed to watercraft.
geocode_display: Albany Beach, Albany, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Gateway to the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta, with a beach launch about a quarter mile from parking. Wheeled kayak carts loaned free from the Visitor Center. Calm, island-dotted Delta channels.
geocode_display: Big Break Regional Shoreline, 69 Big Break Rd, Oakley, CA 94561

notes: Protected inner basin shielded from open Bay wind, with a long pier acting as a windbreak. Launch from the public beach. More ambitious routes include a crossing to Angel Island or south toward Emeryville.
geocode_display: Berkeley Marina, Berkeley, Alameda County, California, 94710, United States

notes: Seven-mile reservoir with a 10 mph speed limit for all vessels, keeping it unusually calm for its size. Launch from the East Beach ramp; a launch and inspection fee applies on top of parking. Summer water is warm enough to skip the wetsuit.
geocode_display: Del Valle Regional Park, Livermore, Alameda County, California, 94550, United States

notes: A flat, protected urban channel with a free public dock at the Jack London Aquatic Center, about half a mile east of the square. Stays calm regardless of Bay conditions, a reliable option when wind shuts down other spots. Estuary Park at the Jack London Aquatic Center is the dedicated small-craft launch on this stretch, with a wide ADA gangway and low-freeboard dock used by kayakers, rowers, and dragon-boat crews.
geocode_display: Oakland, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Shallow, protected tidal basin with access at Tidewater in Oakland and the boat ramp at the end of Grand Avenue in Alameda. The MLK Jr. Shoreline launch opens up the airport channel. Stick to mid or high tide, since mudflats can trap you at low water.
geocode_display: San Leandro Bay, East Oakland, Oakland, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Former gravel quarry with electric-motor-only rules that keep the water smooth. Warm, calm, and one of the most beginner-friendly flatwater spots around.
geocode_display: Shadow Cliffs Regional Recreation Area, 2500 Stanley Blvd, Pleasanton, CA 94566

notes: A wide sandy bay beach on the Bay Water Trail, with a steep quarter-mile path from roadside parking down to the water, so bring a wheeled dolly. Check water quality advisories before you go, especially within three days of rain. Two Point Richmond alternatives sit close by: Ferry Point, the other beach in Miller/Knox Regional Shoreline, has an accessible beach path, a kayak drying rack, and free parking, while Brickyard Cove's Boat Ramp Street offers a hard concrete ramp for easier launching but only a few spaces and no restrooms.
geocode_display: Keller Beach, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, United States

notes: Open San Pablo Bay access with a quarter-mile walk from parking to the Pinole Point put-in. Low tides expose wide mudflats that make launching and landing very difficult, so check the chart. Stay at least 330 feet from harbor seal haul-outs.
geocode_display: Point Pinole Regional Shoreline, 5551, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, 94806, United States

notes: Sheltered inner harbor with a straightforward launch into San Pablo Bay. The calm basin is forgiving, a solid fallback when wind makes exposed spots unpleasant. Restrooms and parking right at the water. The Marina Bay shoreline has more hand-launch points on the same protected basin: Shimada Friendship Park and Vincent Park both put in via short stairs or small beaches steps from free parking, and Marina Bay Yacht Harbor has an ADA low-freeboard dock with SUP handrails inside the wind-sheltered inner basin.
geocode_display: Marina Bay, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, 94804, United States

notes: An oxbow lake with a shore launch and a connection to the Mokelumne River to extend the paddle. The river from the fish hatchery is beginner-friendly with no significant rapids, though sections get shallow by late summer.
geocode_display: Lodi Lake, Lodi, San Joaquin County, California, United States

notes: Top wildlife paddle: sea otters, harbor seals, shorebirds. Launch from Kirby Park or Moss Landing Harbor. Start on a flood tide so you're not fighting the current under the Highway 1 bridge on the way back.
geocode_display: Elkhorn Slough, Moss Landing, Monterey County, California, United States

notes: Marina launch into Richardson Bay. Check tide and wind: it gets very shallow and muddy at low tide and choppy after 10am most days. Launch at mid to high tide.
geocode_display: Loch Lomond Marina, San Rafael, CA

notes: Three launch ramps for windsurfers, kiteboarders, and paddlers along 149 acres of Bay shoreline. The Promenade renovation added a sheltered bay and sandy beach for a calmer inside option.

Crescent surf beach at the mouth of San Pedro Valley off Highway 1, locally known as Linda Mar. Protected southern end is good for beginners launching off the gradual slope. Best at mid-to-high rising tide when the shore break stays small. Rentals available from surf shops across the highway.

hidden_reason: 2026-07-16 audit: no public launch ramp could be confirmed to exist. City of Brisbane's marina page lists no ramp; DBW types the facility Marina, not Launch; SFBAMA's launch guide never mentions Brisbane or Sierra Point; not a Water Trail trailhead; OSM maps no slipway at Sierra Point. The stored coordinate is byte-identical to an unsourced fishing.org entry that itself asserts no physical ramp, and it lands on the Sierra Point Parkway roadway. Hidden pending a harbormaster call (650-583-6975) or an owner-supplied pin. See reports/coord-audit-2026-07-16.md.
notes: Public concrete ramp at Sierra Point with direct access to open SF Bay and a 300-foot fishing pier nearby. The Bay Water Trail runs adjacent. Wind and chop build by midday, so go early.

A paved put-in inside Don Edwards National Wildlife Refuge, with up to 10 miles of protected slough paddling through shorebird and waterfowl habitat. No inspection required. Time the flood tide. An endangered saltmarsh harvest mouse lives in the adjacent vegetation, so stay on the water.

hidden_reason: 2026-07-16 audit: no designated or legally sanctioned public put-in could be found on Coyote Creek off McCarthy Boulevard in FWS, Santa Clara Valley Water, City of Milpitas, County Parks, or SF Bay Water Trail sources; OSM has no slipway within 6km; the stored coordinate reverse-geocodes to the Nimitz Freeway. The matching address is a hiking and biking trailhead. The one documented paddle from it was a permit-only trip into a normally closed section of Don Edwards NWR, whose own trip report tells readers not to paddle there because it is unsafe, illegal, and disruptive to wildlife. The notes also cite the wrong closure months and the wrong species. Hidden on safety and legal grounds pending lawyer-gate review. See reports/coord-audit-2026-07-16.md.
notes: Baylands access off McCarthy Boulevard into tidal salt marsh connecting to Don Edwards Refuge and Alviso Slough. Tidal velocity runs around 1.5 mph with a 9-to-10-foot range, so timing with the flood is critical. Some sections close March through August for heron nesting.
An 83-acre lake in Fremont's Central Park with a dedicated carry-down launch. No motorized boats, so conditions stay consistently smooth for beginners.

A rare urban paddle on a tidal lagoon in the heart of Oakland, ringed by parks and grand architecture. Put in at the Lake Merritt Boating Center (Sailboat House), the only public non-motorized launch on the lake. Bring your own board. Dogs on leash in Lakeside Park.

Former quarries turned into clear freshwater lakes, with a roughly 3-mile loop around Horseshoe Lake. A boat launch dock near the park entrance makes access easy. Mussel inspection required before launching.

ADA-accessible paddle craft dock on San Leandro Bay with a free two-lane launch off Doolittle Drive and open estuary access. Strong birdwatching along the restored wetlands by the ramp. Tidewater Boating Center on site offers rowing and kayak programs.

Reservoir with a designated cartop and paddleboard launch. Surrounding hills feel quiet with minimal powerboat traffic. SUP and windsurf users must shower at least two minutes before entering the water, per Contra Costa Water District rules.

A Bay Water Trail site on the San Joaquin River near its confluence with the Sacramento, with ADA kayak launch facilities and on-dock storage racks. Tidal and wind conditions require intermediate skills, especially in summer afternoon westerlies.

24-hour public launch on Dutch Slough with dock ramps on both sides, into the Delta's backwater channels. The narrow sloughs toward Sand Mound and Franks Tract offer protected flatwater when winds are calm.
geocode_display: Viking Harbor / Dutch Slough Boat Launch, 2140 Dutch Slough Rd, Oakley, CA 94561

notes: A narrow, rocky beach in a 19-acre Marin park, with a dedicated kayak and canoe launch near the fishing pier. The crossing to Angel Island is about 2.5 miles and often passes harbor seals.

Sheltered tidal creek launch next to the Larkspur Landing ferry terminal, with about 2 miles of meandering slough shielded from bay winds. Mind the tide to avoid shallows near the Bon Air Road turnaround.

This is 101 Surf Sports' own dock, a private business dock rather than a public launch, so confirm access with the shop before counting on it. Calm canal water feeds into San Pablo Bay about a mile out. Rentals and board storage on-site. Wind can pick up on the open bay section, so mornings are calmer.
hidden_reason: Delisted by owner (D14, 2026-07-17). Coordinate is 101 Surf Sports' private business dock, not a public launch; a paddler who drives here may have no right to put in (data-quality-sweep-2026-07-16.md row 0). Un-hide only if replaced with a Water-Trail-confirmed public launch on the San Rafael Canal.

notes: A brackish coastal lagoon in Point Reyes, reached by carrying your board 1.1 miles from the Pierce Point Road trailhead. Calm and wildlife-rich, with the ocean just beyond the dunes. No dogs on the trail; nesting restrictions apply March through September.

Park on the spit between ocean and Bodega Harbor, with an ADA-accessible kayak launch and a full trailer boat ramp. The harbor side is calm; the outer bay has harbor seals on rocky outcroppings. Parking and launch require the $7 day-use fee.

California's largest natural freshwater lake, with a dedicated boat ramp inside the state park on Soda Bay Road. Sheltered coves near the park are calm and beginner-friendly, and water runs noticeably warmer than Bay Area reservoirs.
SF's largest freshwater lake, ringed by a 4.5-mile paved trail and free of tides and currents. One of the calmest, most predictable paddles in the city.

Wide sandy beach in the Presidio launching straight onto the Bay. Wind and tide-driven currents are common, so it suits intermediate and experienced paddlers; mornings before the afternoon westerlies are the sweet spot.

California's first urban state park, launching from Jackrabbit Beach on the southeastern corner of the Bay; the sandy launch needs a higher tide for good access. Draws windsurfers plus kayak and SUP paddlers wanting a less-crowded city put-in. Dogs on leash throughout the park.
Where the American meets the Sacramento, with a six-lane boat ramp and access to two rivers. The American River Parkway bike trail runs alongside for a paddle-and-ride day. Dogs must be on leash.

Nimbus Flat sits on Lake Natoma, the cool, calm reservoir below Folsom Dam, where a 5 mph motor limit keeps the water glassy. It is home to the Sacramento State Aquatic Center, which rents boards and boats. A paved bike trail runs the length of the lake, and Negro Bar is another nearby put-in. $12 parking, no additional launch fee.
The most popular beach access on Folsom Lake, with four concrete launch ramps and ample sandy shore for hand-launching. $12 parking; hand-launched boats have no additional launch fee.

Crystal-clear alpine paddling just off I-80, ringed by granite peaks and pine. A public ramp on the northwest shore and 37 first-come public docks make launching easy. Self-inspection mandatory; power boats allowed with a 35 mph limit.
Largest public sandy beach on Tahoe's north shore, 1,700 feet of lakefront. The Coon Street launch ramp is non-motorized only. Aquatic invasive species inspection required before launching.

At 7,414 feet in El Dorado National Forest, gateway to Desolation Wilderness, with deep blue water framed by granite. Echo Chalet handles launching. The 4.5-mile round-trip across Lower and Upper Echo Lakes is a classic Sierra paddle. Invasive species inspection required.
A wind-swept alpine reservoir at 7,000 feet, prized by sailors and paddlers. Launch from the public ramp between College and Deer Creek Campgrounds. Afternoon winds pick up fast, so start early.
Lake at 5,370 feet in the Sierra National Forest, with access at the north-shore launch ramp and Camp Edison marina. Afternoon winds develop, so mornings are best.

Sprawling El Dorado National Forest lake via Ice House Road, with three free ramps at Sunset, Yellowjacket, and Westpoint campgrounds. Calm forested shoreline, great for beginners. Water skiing and wake boats also use the lake, so keep to the coves.
A vast Gold Country reservoir with miles of quiet coves and arms. The Glory Hole Recreation Area provides a boat launch. Rental delivery is available right at the ramp.
Foothill reservoir with paved launch ramps at Fleming Meadows and Moccasin Point. Warm water and canyon scenery. Mornings are calmest; afternoon wind is common across the main lake.

Warm-water reservoir in the Calaveras foothills with four launch ramps and miles of quiet coves. Carry-down walkways and boarding floats available. Verify ramp conditions before visiting, as invasive mussel protocols occasionally close individual ramps.

A county reservoir north of Oakdale; paddling allowed within 200 feet of shore. Launch from the Bayview Boat Ramp, with a 5 mph zone over most arms. Water contact is restricted Labor Day through May, so visit in the warmer season.
Protected estuary where Coleman Park's small beach gives the easiest entry at any tide level. Sea otters, harbor seals, and shorebirds are common throughout the bay.

Old Port Beach at Port San Luis is a drive-to launch where at low tide you can roll gear straight to the waterline. The protected cove is calmer and warmer than most Central Coast spots, with views from Point Sal to Point San Luis. Avila Beach Paddlesports runs rentals and tours on the harbor.

A calm tidal lagoon and wetlands reserve with marshy channels thick with shorebirds, at Carmel River State Beach. Short portage from the lot to the water. Tides affect lagoon depth, and the ocean mouth area can be hazardous.

water: Willow Creek Recreation Area - Lake Natoma
notes: Calm, wind-sheltered launch on the western end of non-motorized Lake Natoma, good for beginners and easy mornings. The reservoir runs cool below Folsom Dam, and a paved bike trail follows the shoreline. Paved ramp, restrooms, covered picnic tables. Day-use fee is $10 (Poppy Pass / Golden Poppy Annual Pass accepted). Access from westbound Folsom Blvd between Blue Ravine Rd and Natoma Station Drive.
geocode_display: Willow Creek Recreation Area, 13303 Folsom Blvd, Folsom, CA 95630

notes: Public boat ramps and floating docks at the downtown waterfront, with direct access to Suisun Slough and the marsh channels. Check tides and wind: mudflats expose at low tide and afternoon wind makes the paddle back tough.
geocode_display: Suisun City Marina, Kellogg Street, Suisun City, CA

notes: Boat ramp on Montezuma Slough at the edge of the Grizzly Island marsh, with a paved lot and restrooms. Prime birdwatching through the sloughs. Tidal: check the current and watch for mudflats at low tide.
geocode_display: Belden's Landing, Grizzly Island Road, Suisun City, CA

notes: Working marina at the tip of the Point San Pablo peninsula, with launch access into San Pablo Bay near the Richmond-San Rafael Bridge. Open water catches afternoon wind and tidal current, so hug the shoreline when it builds. Quiet weekday paddling past the old whaling-station coast.
geocode_display: Point San Pablo Yacht Harbor, Stenmark Drive, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, United States

notes: Alpine lake at about 7,200 feet on Highway 88 in Amador County. Cold, clear water ringed by granite and pine, with easy paddleboard put-ins along the shore. Campgrounds on both sides of the lake. Afternoon mountain wind is common, so paddle early.
geocode_display: Silver Lake, Kit Carson, Amador County, California, United States

notes: Large reservoir northeast of Sacramento with several launch points; Beals Point has a ramp, beach, and parking. Power boats share the main body, so wake builds on warm afternoons, and open fetch lets wind kick up quickly. State park day-use fee at the entrance.
geocode_display: Beals Point, Folsom Lake State Recreation Area, Folsom, California, United States

notes: Former gravel quarry lake in Riverfront Regional Park along the Russian River near Healdsburg. The northeast corner has the easiest put-in. Quiet, flat water shaded by valley oaks and redwoods. Sonoma County park day-use fee.
geocode_display: Riverfront Regional Park, Wilson Grove, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Protected harbor launch with calm water inside the breakwater and quick access to Monterey Bay. SUP Shack rents boards and launches from the boat docks, and Kayak Connection rents nearby. Sea otters and sea lions are common around the harbor and out along the kelp.
geocode_display: Santa Cruz Small Craft Harbor, Seabright, Santa Cruz, Santa Cruz County, California, United States

notes: Free public boat launch at the foot of West 9th Street, with a fishing pier, restrooms, and picnic and BBQ areas. Launch point onto the Carquinez Strait toward Benicia State Recreation Area. Winds and currents run strong and large ships use the shipping lanes offshore, so keep close to shore and pick calm conditions.
geocode_display: 9th Street Boat Ramp, West 9th Street, Benicia, Solano County, California, United States

notes: Calm, narrow flatwater on Putah Creek below Monticello Dam. No gas or electric motors are allowed, so the water stays glassy and beginner-friendly. The water is very cold year-round because it is released from the bottom of Lake Berryessa. Free launch for hand-carried and car-top craft (day-use fee applies), with picnic sites and restrooms. Quiet, shaded, and rich with birdlife, and well known for fly fishing.
geocode_display: Lake Solano Park, 8685 Pleasant Valley Road, Winters, Solano County, California, United States

notes: Open-bay destination in the middle of San Francisco Bay, reachable only by boat. Land on a beach or pull up to the seawall and dinghy slips at Ayala Cove, which has restrooms, a visitor center, cafe, and bike rentals; a state park day-use fee applies. The hazards are serious: strong currents through Raccoon Strait and off China Cove, wind around Point Blunt, and heavy ferry, shipping, and motorboat traffic. Only experienced paddlers should make the crossing.
geocode_display: Angel Island State Park, Tiburon, California, United States

notes: Sheltered launch on the J Channel slough where the Bay meets the Delta, with access to the San Joaquin and Sacramento Rivers, Honker Bay, and Suisun Marsh. An ADA low-freeboard dock holds water at all tides, about 1,000 feet from the lot, so a boat dolly helps. Paved parking, accessible restrooms with potable water, and picnic areas are on site. The channel is calm and good for less experienced paddlers, but mudflats expose at low tide and the open river brings strong currents, wind, and commercial vessel traffic.
geocode_display: Bay Point Regional Shoreline, Bay Point, California, United States

notes: A windsurfing and kiteboarding shoreline on the bay with consistent strong wind. Reach the water by a steep entry path near the turf rigging area to the north, or a ramp to a small sandy beach to the south, with a third beach about 0.3 mile along the Bay Trail. A large paved lot, ADA restrooms, washdown station, and an on-site boardsailing concession with instruction and rentals are here. Winds and currents run strong, and the steep ramps make water entry hard for hand-carried craft.
geocode_display: Baywinds Park, Foster City, California, United States

notes: Popular public launch at the mouth of the Petaluma River, looking out to San Pablo Bay. Put in from the boat ramp or high-freeboard dock, with a boat-washing hose and fish-cleaning station on site. Two lots, picnic tables, and ADA restrooms with running water are provided, with a $5 day-use parking fee and sunrise-to-sunset hours. The river is tidal, so currents and wind can be strong. Avoid landing in the marshes to protect sensitive rail habitat.
geocode_display: Black Point Boat Launch, Novato, California, United States

notes: Launch into the shallow restored tidal marsh of the San Pablo Bay National Wildlife Refuge, with sloughs and the Napa River about 3.5 miles west along Dutchman Slough. An ADA gangway leads to a low-freeboard dock with two kayak berths, roughly 250 feet from a small eight-space lot with a portable restroom. The launch stays navigable at all tides, but extensive mudflats hide hazards at low water and currents run strong at the slough breaches. Seasonal hunting occurs nearby and endangered species use the refuge, so keep your distance from wildlife.
geocode_display: Cullinan Ranch Boat Launch, Vallejo, California, United States

notes: Scenic rural launch on the Napa River that opens onto the Napa-Sonoma Marshes, popular for paddling and fishing. A wide boat ramp is flanked by two high-freeboard docks that run parallel to shore. A large free lot (4AM to 10PM, no overnight), ADA restrooms without running water, and picnic tables with a gazebo are on site. The river is tidal, seasonal hunting occurs in parts of the marsh, and boaters should stay out of narrow channels to protect sensitive habitat.
geocode_display: Cuttings Wharf, Napa, California, United States

notes: Remote tidal-marsh restoration launch in the Sears Point unit of the San Pablo Bay National Wildlife Refuge, prized for birdwatching and open views. A compacted gravel ramp sits about a quarter mile from parking, so a boat dolly helps and the site is most often used on guided tours. A large gravel lot with two paved ADA spaces, a portable restroom, and miles of Bay Trail are here. The ramp stays navigable at all tides, but extensive mudflats hide underwater hazards at low water.
geocode_display: Dickson Ranch Boat Launch, Novato, California, United States

notes: Destination dock on the Napa River fronting downtown Napa's riverfront, meant for tying up rather than launching, since paddlers put in elsewhere on the river. A high-freeboard dock has nine cleats plus a seasonal low-freeboard kayak dock from April to October, open 7AM to 11PM with a three-hour limit and no overnight. The accessible gangway runs about 112 feet with two rest platforms, and public restrooms are nearby downtown. The river is tidal with strong winter currents and shared with motorboats, so stay along the sides and out of the main channel in heavy traffic.
geocode_display: Downtown Napa - Main Street Dock, Napa, California, United States

notes: A newer public launch on tidal Mt Eden Creek in a 6,400-acre salt marsh reserve. An ADA gangway leads to a high-freeboard dock with a low-float attachment for non-motorized boats, with a turf rigging area at the top. About 30 free parking spaces sit a quarter mile away, with a portable restroom and miles of Bay Trail. The creek stays navigable at all tides, but extensive mudflats at its mouth dry out at low tide, so check the tide chart first. On about ten hunt dates between November and January the reserve closes to everyone except registered hunters, so check CDFW's schedule before a winter trip. A stormwater pipe near the launch can create a hazard. Landing along the shoreline is not allowed: it is nesting habitat for Ridgway's rail and the snowy plover.
geocode_display: Eden Landing Ecological Reserve, Hayward, California, United States

notes: A marina launch onto open bay water with sweeping Bay Bridge and Golden Gate views, shared by motorized and non-motorized boats. Put in from the cement ramp and high-freeboard dock, or use the boardsailer water-access path and rinse station just outside the breakwater. Free four-hour and paid parking, picnic areas, and restrooms are on site, and a kiteboarding outfitter offers seasonal lessons and rentals. Winds and currents can be strong, and the shallow Emeryville Crescent to the south should be avoided for its mudflats and sensitive habitat.
geocode_display: Emeryville Marina, Emeryville, California, United States

notes: A protected-water landing on Islais Creek in San Francisco's southern industrial waterfront. Launch from the gravel beach or the high-freeboard dock reached by an ADA gangway. There are 18 public spaces on Arthur Avenue plus street parking, a creekside park with benches and picnic tables, and a portable restroom when the Kayaks Unlimited co-op is on site. The main hazard is marine industrial traffic near the creek mouth, so give ships, barges, and tugs wide berth and the right of way.
geocode_display: Islais Creek, San Francisco, California, United States

notes: A ramp-and-dock launch on the tidal Petaluma River, navigable 1.5 miles upriver to the downtown Turning Basin and about 12 remote miles downriver toward San Pablo Bay. Put in from the boat ramp or high-freeboard dock, open sunrise to sunset, with a boat-washing hose on site. Ample parking sits near the launch, with restrooms at the harbormaster office plus picnic tables, a hotel, and restaurants. The river is tidal and winds and currents can be strong.
geocode_display: Petaluma Marina, Petaluma, California, United States

notes: A public launch in the heart of downtown Petaluma on the tidal Petaluma River, navigable downriver to San Pablo Bay. Put in from Cavanagh Landing, a large U-shaped dock reached by gangways from the River Plaza or Weller Street sides. Parking is limited to a few spots in the Weller Street lot plus street parking, with restaurants, breweries, and shops steps away. The river is tidal with sometimes strong winds and currents and occasional barge traffic. A paddlesports shop a few blocks away offers rentals and lessons.
geocode_display: Petaluma River Turning Basin, Petaluma, California, United States

notes: A marina on the tidal San Joaquin and Sacramento Rivers with bay access to the west and the Delta to the east, used by motorized and non-motorized boats. A dedicated small-boat slip has a low-float EZ launch with guide rails, rollers, and a SUP grab bar, reached by an ADA gangway and open on request 8AM to 5PM. Large free parking, restrooms, and a waterfront restaurant are on site, and the city rents kayaks and SUPs. Winds and currents can be strong, especially in summer, and small craft must watch for barges and ships in the shipping lanes.
geocode_display: Pittsburg Marina, Pittsburg, California, United States

notes: A kiteboarding and windsurfing launch on the San Joaquin and Sacramento Rivers, just east of the Pittsburg Marina, known for consistent wind. Launch from two ramps over a riprap levee, with rigging space and self-launch assist posts on the wide levee top. The four-acre park has roughly 50 parking spaces, restrooms with an outdoor shower, lawns, a playground, and picnic areas. The levee launch leaves little margin for error and suits experienced riders only.
geocode_display: Riverview Park, Pittsburg, California, United States

notes: A scenic launch next to Marina Green and Crissy Field, near the Golden Gate, managed by SF Recreation and Parks. An ADA gangway leads to a low-float EZ launch with guide rails, rollers, and a transfer bench, but the dock gate is locked, so check out a free key card from the marina office. Free parking 6AM to 10PM, restrooms, lawns, and trails are nearby. Conditions outside the marina turn hazardous fast with strong winds, currents, and shipping traffic, so stay near shore and do not attempt the Golden Gate without experience.
geocode_display: San Francisco Marina (Gashouse Cove), San Francisco, California, United States

notes: A California state park on the tidal Delta near the Sacramento and San Joaquin confluence, with a multi-lane boat launch and protected sloughs to paddle. Amenities include over 100 campsites, picnic areas, a swimming beach, restrooms, and trailer parking. Strong afternoon Delta wind, tidal current, and busy motorboat traffic are the main hazards on the open water.
geocode_display: Brannan Island State Recreation Area, Rio Vista, California, United States

notes: Calm tidal sloughs and flatwater channels through a protected wetland known for abundant birdlife, including wintering sandhill cranes. Access and parking are free, and paddlers hand-carry kayaks, canoes, or SUPs about 200 yards from the upper lot to the launch. Motorized boats are not permitted, so the water stays quiet, though currents can run fast after winter storms.
geocode_display: Cosumnes River Preserve, Galt, California, United States

notes: A 53-acre Stockton city park on the San Joaquin Deep Water Channel with a public launch ramp, docks, ample parking, restrooms, picnic tables, and a playground. The tidal Delta channel here carries ocean-going ships and motorboats, so wake and vessel traffic are real hazards. Strong afternoon Delta wind and tidal current add to the conditions.
geocode_display: Buckley Cove Park & Boat Launch, Stockton, California, United States

water: Isleton Public Dock and Boat Ramp
notes: A small public launch on the tidal Sacramento River in the Delta town of Isleton, with a boat ramp and dock right off Second Street. It puts you onto open river water shared with motorboats and subject to daily tidal swings. Strong afternoon Delta wind and passing boat wake are the main things to watch.
geocode_display: Isleton Public Dock and Boat Ramp, Isleton, California, United States

notes: A free public dock on the tidal Sacramento River in the small Delta town of Walnut Grove. There is no trailer ramp, so it works best as a hand launch for kayaks, canoes, and SUPs straight off the dock. The river is shared with motorboats and rises and falls with the tide, and afternoon Delta wind can build quickly.
geocode_display: Walnut Grove Public Dock, Walnut Grove, California, United States

notes: A wildlife area access at the Sacramento and San Joaquin confluence, renowned for windsurfing and kiteboarding because of the powerful Delta breeze. Paddlers hand launch from the access road into open, exposed water, with restrooms and primitive day use nearby. Expect very strong wind, heavy tidal current, and large motorboat traffic, which make this one of the most demanding launches in the Delta.
geocode_display: Lower Sherman Island Fishing Access, Rio Vista, California, United States

notes: A small neighborhood lake inside Lakeshore Park. A dock off Chelsea Drive serves as the put-in. No restrooms at the park. Fishing is allowed.
Features a launch ramp, beach area, and docks. Located on the Carquinez Strait with views of the Benicia–Martinez Bridge.
geocode_display: Martinez Marina, Tarantino Drive, Martinez, Contra Costa County, California, 94510, United States

notes: A dedicated concrete boat ramp drops straight from the parking lot to the water, so the carry is short and easy. Through summer the Russian River here runs high and settles into a lake-like calm, one of the mellower flatwater stretches around Guerneville.
Quiet-water beach inside the Marina del Rey basin with a dedicated hand launch for kayaks and paddleboards. Parking is paid. Restrooms on site.
geocode_display: Marina del Rey, Los Angeles County, California, United States

notes: Hand launch inside the harbor, alongside four marinas and boating services. Parking is paid and restrooms are on site.
geocode_display: King Harbor Marina, Redondo Beach, Los Angeles County, California, United States

notes: Launch ramp at the north end of Cabrillo Beach, on the sheltered inner harbor side rather than the open ocean side. Dogs are not allowed.
geocode_display: Cabrillo Beach, San Pedro, Los Angeles, California, United States

notes: Protected bay beach on Alamitos Bay, also known as Mother's Beach. Metered parking and restrooms on site.
geocode_display: Mother's Beach, Long Beach, Los Angeles County, California, United States

notes: Sheltered dredged basin off Appian Way, long used for rowing and flatwater paddling. Paddling is permitted outside sanctioned events. Paid parking and restrooms on site.
geocode_display: Marine Stadium, Long Beach, Los Angeles County, California, United States

notes: Calm bay beach on the Bayshore side of Alamitos Bay. The sand runs a long way, so there is no single put-in and you can launch anywhere along it. Free, with parking and restrooms.
geocode_display: Bayshore Beach, Alamitos Bay, Long Beach, Los Angeles County, California, United States

notes: Bay beach on Mission Bay with a small craft launch. Small boats are staged on the sand at the southeast shore of the point. Parking and restrooms on site.
Kayak launch on the sheltered east side of Mission Bay, off East Mission Bay Drive. Parking and restrooms on site.

Hand launch into a sheltered cove on the San Diego Bay side of the Silver Strand, at the Crown Cove Aquatic Center. The open ocean is on the far side of the strand, so the cove itself stays protected. Entry is via Silver Strand State Beach.

Sheltered tidal lagoon used for kayaking, paddling and birding, with a public launch on the lagoon shore. A Carlsbad city permit is required to be on the water, and launching from the concessionaire beach costs extra on top.

The best known kayak launch in San Diego, and an open ocean beach: surf breaks the length of it, so the carry in and the return through the break are the hard parts. Wide sand, large parking lot and restrooms.
Launch ramp inside Oceanside Harbor, a small craft harbor with restaurants and services around it.
Launch ramp on Mission Bay at Santa Clara Place, with tennis courts and a ball field alongside.
Launch ramp and marina on the south side of Mission Bay.
Launch ramp on Mission Bay off Ingraham Street. The bay here is used for water skiing, so expect powered traffic.
Launch ramp at the north end of Mission Bay, with swimming nearby and an RV park and golf course adjacent.
Launch ramp on the south shore of Mission Bay, off Sea World Drive.
Launch ramp on San Diego Bay with a paved shoreline path running alongside.
Launch ramp in a working harbor used by commercial and sport fishing boats and whale watching tours.
Launch ramp on Glorietta Bay, on the sheltered Coronado side of San Diego Bay.
Launch ramp at the south end of Tidelands Avenue on San Diego Bay, with a marina adjacent.
Launch ramp on San Diego Bay at the west end of Marina Way, next to Chula Vista Bayfront Park.

Sand strip where Channel Place Park meets the harbor channel on Newport Island, with a ballfield and open lawn behind it. Restrooms on site, no dedicated parking lot, so it is street parking in a dense neighborhood.
Small bayfront beach on the Lido Isle channel in Newport Harbor. No dedicated parking.
Bayside beach at the end of 10th Street on the Balboa Peninsula, on the sheltered harbor side rather than the ocean side. No dedicated parking.
Street-end bayside beach on the harbor side of the Balboa Peninsula. No dedicated parking.
County beach on the sheltered inner side of Newport Harbor, near the harbor entrance. Parking and restrooms on site.
Small cove beach tucked inside the Newport Harbor entrance at Corona del Mar. No parking lot and no restrooms, and the approach is down through a steep residential street.

Crescent of sand inside Dana Point Harbor, fully behind the breakwater, so the water stays flat with no surf. This is the calmest launch in the county and the usual place people learn. Parking, restrooms and a park behind the beach; a marina sits immediately east.

Coastal lagoon separated from the ocean by a barrier beach, so the water is flat and there is no surf to get through. Parking and restrooms at the visitor center pullout on Highway 101. Being a closed lagoon rather than an inlet, it does not run on the tide.

Winding tidal slough through the salt marsh at the south end of Humboldt Bay, inside the national wildlife refuge. Sheltered water and heavy bird life. The bay here is shallow and drains to mudflat on a big low, so the tide decides how far you get. Parking and restrooms at the trailhead.

Small beach tucked beside Capitola Wharf, on the sheltered curve of Monterey Bay rather than the open coast. There is still a shore break to time on the way in and out. Parking nearby, no restrooms at the beach itself.

Hand launch beside the marina at the south end of Morro Bay, away from the busier waterfront. Protected estuary water with the back bay and its eelgrass beds immediately south. Much of that back bay dries to mudflat at low tide, so check the tide before planning a long loop. Parking and restrooms on site.

The launch here is the small protected cove on the harbor side of the park, not the ocean beach on the other side, which takes the full swell and breaks along its whole length. The cove itself stays calm. Parking, restrooms and a playground in the park.
